pathogen_scientific_name.quote: Gray mold (Botrytis blight) caused by the fungus Botrytis cinerea .

type_of_disease.quote: Botrytis blight or “gray mold” is a widely distributed disease caused by the fungus Botrytis cinerea .

affected_parts.quote: The disease can cause spotting and decay of flowers, leaves, fruits, and berries. On some plants, such as roses, it can cause slightly sunken areas called cankers on the stems. Corms and bulbs of perennials and annuals may rot when infected with gray mold.

summary.value: Gray-brown velvety mold covers stems or leaves of young plants; gray spores cover dying flowers and the calyx of fruit, with stem cankers that may girdle and cause wilting above the infected area.
summary.quote: Gray mold appears on young plants as gray-brown velvety mold covering stems or leaves. Infections that girdle the stem cause wilting above the infected area. In the field gray spores cover dying flowers and the calyx of fruit.

diagnostic_features.value: Under a hand lens, the spore-bearing structures resemble bunches of grapes; small green fruit infected directly by airborne spores develop white, circular rings called 'ghost spots'; infected fruit in storage shows the typical gray coating of spores and may have a white mycelium on the surface.
diagnostic_features.quote: Under a hand lens, the spore-bearing structures resemble bunches of grapes... Small green fruit infected directly by airborne spores instead of by contact with other infections usually does not decay, but develops white, circular rings called "'ghost spots."' Infected fruit held in storage at high humidity often decays and shows the typical gray coating of spores; it may also have a white mycelium on the surface.

conflicts.note: Clemson uses parenthetical synonym 'Botrytis Blight'; UC IPM uses simply 'Gray Mold'.
UC IPM lists foliar, stem, and fruit/calyx (pod) only; Clemson adds whole-plant impacts including corms/bulbs.

type_of_disease.quote: Phytophthora infestans  is a member of the oomycetes, a group of organisms sometimes referred to as the "water molds"

affected_parts.quote: Late blight is a potentially devastating disease of tomato and potato, infecting leaves, stems, tomato fruit, and potato tubers.

summary.value: Late blight is identified by blackish/brown lesions on leaves and stems that may be small at first and appear water-soaked or have chlorotic borders but expand rapidly until the entire leaf becomes necrotic; in humid conditions white sporulation appears at lesion margins on lower leaf surfaces. Infected tubers show copper brown, reddish or purplish discoloration and tomato fruit develop dark brown, firm lesions.
summary.quote: Late blight of potato is identified by blackish/brown lesions on leaves and stems ( Figures 3,4 ) that may be small at first and appear water-soaked or have chlorotic borders but expand rapidly and the entire leaf becomes become necrotic. In humid conditions,  P. infestans  produces sporangia and sporangiophores on the surface of infected tissue and the resulting white sporulation can be seen at the margins of lesions on abaxial (lower) surfaces of leaves

diagnostic_features.value: White sporulation (sporangia and sporangiophores) of P. infestans visible at margins of lesions on the abaxial (lower) surfaces of leaves in humid conditions; blackish/brown lesions with water-soaked appearance or chlorotic borders; infected tubers show copper brown, reddish or purplish internal discoloration.
diagnostic_features.quote: the resulting white sporulation can be seen at the margins of lesions on abaxial (lower) surfaces of leaves ( Figures 5 )... Infected tuber tissues ( Figures 7,8 ) are copper brown, reddish or purplish in color.

look_alikes.quote: Symptoms of late blight infections on tomato fruit is similar to other Phytophthora diseases

conflicts.alternative_value: Late blight of potato and tomato
conflicts.note: APS uses the long form naming both hosts; UMN and Cornell use 'Late blight'.
Cornell focuses on leaves, stems, and tomato fruit; UMN and APS additionally cite potato tubers (root organ).

conflicts.canonical_value: APS Education Center description (selected as highest-rank source)
conflicts.alternative_value: Leaves develop large dark brown blotches with green-gray edges; stems show firm dark brown lesions; tomato fruit develop firm dark brown circular spots; potato tubers become discolored, shrunken and soft. In high humidity, thin powdery white fungal growth appears on infected tissue.
conflicts.note: UMN emphasizes 'green-gray edges' on leaf blotches not confined by major veins; APS emphasizes water-soaked / chlorotic borders. Both descriptions are consistent but use different focal features.

pathogen_scientific_name.quote: Cucumber mosaic virus in the cucumovirus group

type_of_disease.quote: CMV is made up of nucleic acid (ribonucleic acid, RNA) surrounded by a protein coat.

affected_parts.quote: Generally, plants appear lighter in color and are bushy and stunted. Close up symptoms include a mosaic (alternating light and dark green areas) on at least some leaves, especially on the younger leaves.

summary.value: Plants appear lighter in color, bushy and stunted, with mosaic patterns of alternating light and dark green areas on leaves (especially younger leaves); leaves may exhibit a shoestring-like or strappy appearance, and fruit production is greatly reduced.
summary.quote: Generally, plants appear lighter in color and are bushy and stunted. Close up symptoms include a mosaic (alternating light and dark green areas) on at least some leaves, especially on the younger leaves. Leaves may exhibit a shoestring-like appearance. Fruit production is greatly reduced.

diagnostic_features.value: Leaves may become "strappy" or have a "shoestring" appearance; this is the symptom most identified with CMV.
diagnostic_features.quote: Leaves may become “strappy” or have a “shoestring” appearance; this is the symptom most identified with CMV.

look_alikes.quote: These symptoms can be confused with those caused by tobacco mosaic virus .

conflicts.note: Tomato-specific naming variant of the same disease.

conflicts.alternative_value: Mosaic Virus Diseases Caused by Cucumoviruses
conflicts.note: Group-level naming variant referring to the same CMV pathogen.

pathogen_scientific_name.quote: Early blight of tomato is caused primarily by the fungus Alternaria linariae (= A. tomatophila ; formerly known as A. solani)

type_of_disease.quote: Early blight of tomato is caused primarily by the fungus Alternaria linariae

affected_parts.quote: The leaves, fruit, and stem may be affected by Alternaria linariae .

summary.value: Lesions develop on lower leaves as small, brownish-black spots that expand to 1/4-1/2 inch with characteristic concentric rings; surrounding tissue yellows and entire leaves may turn yellow, with fruit lesions appearing sunken, leathery, and dark brown to black with concentric rings.
summary.quote: Lesions first develop on lower leaves as small, brownish-black spots which can expand to about 1 ⁄ 4 - 1 ⁄ 2 -inch in diameter with characteristic concentric rings in the darkened area. The area surrounding the lesions may become yellow and, as disease progresses, the entire leaf may turn yellow.

diagnostic_features.value: Brownish-black lesions with characteristic concentric rings in the darkened area, primarily on lower leaves; fruit lesions are sunken, leathery, and dark brown to black with concentric rings.
diagnostic_features.quote: small, brownish-black spots which can expand to about 1 ⁄ 4 - 1 ⁄ 2 -inch in diameter with characteristic concentric rings in the darkened area

look_alikes.quote: Septoria leaf spot produces smaller lesions with tan or light gray centers. Gray leaf spot also produces smaller and lighter brown lesions than what you would see with early blight... Late blight produces lighter tan colored lesions that typically have a light green halo... Bacterial spot also may be confused with early blight

diseases.disease_name: Bacterial Canker And Wilt Of Tomato

pathogen_scientific_name.quote: The disease is caused by the bacterial pathogen Clavibacter michiganensis subspecies michiganensis .

type_of_disease.quote: This bacterial disease is suspected to be seedborne, and can survive in solanaceous weeds and decaying plant tissue.

affected_parts.quote: Seedlings Small, white, raised spots may form on leaves. Leaf edge discoloration and wilting. Seedling completely wilts and dies. ... Stem Internal stem discoloration caused by bacterial canker. Brown streaks can be seen in the vascular system when the stem is cut open. Stem splits, forming long, brown cankers... Fruit Fruit infection of bacterial canker Fruit may have small (1/4 inch), raised, white spots with tan or brown centers

summary.value: Symptoms include small white raised spots on leaves, yellow to tan patches between veins, brown leaf edges with yellow border, wilting (often one-sided), brown vascular streaks, stem splits forming brown cankers, and fruit with raised white spots with tan/brown centers (bird's eye spot).
summary.quote: Small, white, raised spots may form on leaves. Leaf edge discoloration and wilting. Seedling completely wilts and dies. ... Yellow to tan patches form between veins. Edges of leaf turn brown, with a yellow border. Veins on the leaves and petioles can become dark and sunken. ... Brown streaks can be seen in the vascular system when the stem is cut open. Stem splits, forming long, brown cankers. Yellow sticky fluid may emerge from cut stem when squeezed. ... Fruit may have small (1/4 inch), raised, white spots with tan or brown centers (called bird's eye spot).

diagnostic_features.value: Fruit shows small (1/4 inch), raised, white spots with tan or brown centers known as 'bird's eye spot'; fruit surface may appear netted or marbled; lower leaves wilt often only on one side; yellow sticky fluid may emerge from cut stem when squeezed.
diagnostic_features.quote: Fruit may have small (1/4 inch), raised, white spots with tan or brown centers (called bird's eye spot). Fruit surface may appear netted or marbled. ... Lower leaves wilt, often only on one side. ... Yellow sticky fluid may emerge from cut stem when squeezed.

pathogen_scientific_name.quote: Bacterial speck is caused by the bacterium Pseudomonas syringae pv. tomato.

type_of_disease.quote: Bacterial speck and spot are common bacterial diseases of tomatoes in the southeastern United States.

affected_parts.quote: Symptoms of bacterial speck can occur on leaves, stems, petioles, peduncles, pedicels, sepals, and fruit.

summary.value: Bacterial speck produces small (1/16 in.) slightly-raised black speck-like lesions on developing fruit and similar lesions on leaves surrounded by faint yellow halos with substantial leaf curling; lesions are dark brown to black and may appear on leaves, fruit, and stems with chlorotic tissue surrounding leaf lesions.
summary.quote: Bacterial speck produces small (1/16 in.) slightly-raised black 'speck-like' lesions on the developing fruit that are much smaller than tomatoes with bacterial spot. On tomato leaves, similar lesions to bacterial spot appear except that faint yellow halos surround the lesions and the leaf may show substantial curling.

diagnostic_features.value: Small (1/16 in.) slightly-raised black 'speck-like' lesions on developing fruit that are much smaller than bacterial spot lesions; leaf lesions surrounded by faint yellow halos with substantial leaf curling. Unlike bacterial canker, no excessive wilting, canker development, or 'bird's-eye spots' on fruit.
diagnostic_features.quote: Bacterial speck produces small (1/16 in.) slightly-raised black 'speck-like' lesions on the developing fruit that are much smaller than tomatoes with bacterial spot. On tomato leaves, similar lesions to bacterial spot appear except that faint yellow halos surround the lesions and the leaf may show substantial curling. In contrast to bacterial speck, bacterial canker will show excessive wilting, canker development, and prominent 'bird's-eye spots' on fruit.

look_alikes.quote: Symptoms associated with bacterial speck and spot on leaflets may also be confused with other diseases of tomatoes, such as early blight, gray leaf spot, and target spot.

conflicts.note: Cornell expands list to include roots and seeds based on entry points (natural openings, wounds, infected seeds), while Missouri and Mississippi State limit symptom-bearing parts to foliar and stem tissues.

conflicts.canonical_value: bacterial spot, bacterial canker, early blight, gray leaf spot, target spot
conflicts.note: Missouri lists only bacterial spot as a look-alike; Cornell adds bacterial canker; Mississippi State adds early blight, gray leaf spot, and target spot. Canonical value is the union across sources.

pathogen_scientific_name.quote: Septoria leaf spot is caused by the fungus Septoria lycopersici .

type_of_disease.quote: Septoria leaf spot is caused by the fungus Septoria lycopersici .

affected_parts.quote: Symptoms generally develop on the lower leaves first and then the disease progresses up the plant. Lesions also develop on stems, petioles, and calyx, although fruit are rarely affected.

summary.value: Circular, tan to gray lesions develop on the lower leaves and stems, typically small (<1/8 in), with a dark-brown perimeter and sometimes a yellow halo; small dark pycnidia may appear in the centers, and on the underside spots may look darker green or water-soaked.
summary.quote: Circular, tan to gray, lesions develop on the lower leaves and stems of the plant. Lesions are typically small (<1/8 in), but as lesions become more numerous the entire leaf may become blighted. The lesions generally have a dark-brown perimeter and a yellow halo may develop around the leaf lesions. Small dark spots may appear in the center of the lesion – these are pycnidia, the fruiting body of the fungus where spores are produced. On the underside of the leaves, spots may appear as a darker green color or "water-soaked."

diagnostic_features.value: Small (<1/8 in) circular tan-to-gray lesions with a dark-brown perimeter, often a yellow halo, and small dark pycnidia visible in the center; symptoms start on lower leaves and progress upward, fruit are rarely affected.
diagnostic_features.quote: Circular, tan to gray, lesions develop on the lower leaves and stems of the plant. Lesions are typically small (<1/8 in)... The lesions generally have a dark-brown perimeter and a yellow halo may develop around the leaf lesions. Small dark spots may appear in the center of the lesion – these are pycnidia, the fruiting body of the fungus where spores are produced.

look_alikes.quote: Lesions caused by the early blight pathogen will be larger and darker brown than Septoria leaf spots and will have concentric rings. Gray leaf spot, caused by species of Stemphylium , will be very similar to Septoria leaf spots where they are small, circular, with a tan or light brown center, but the center part of the lesion tends to crack open or fall out in gray leaf spot lesions.

conflicts.note: Wikipedia uses the shorter form 'Septoria leaf spot'; UW uses 'Tomato Septoria Leaf Spot'.
Wikipedia adds Seed (overwintered mycelium and conidia in pycnidia found on/in infected seed); NC State and UW emphasize Foliar + Stem.

conflicts.canonical_value: NC State description (tan-to-gray, <1/8 in, dark-brown perimeter, yellow halo, pycnidia)
conflicts.alternative_value: Wikipedia: 2–5 mm diameter, greyish center with brown margins, leaves yellow then brown, shrivel and drop off
conflicts.note: Wikipedia provides a metric size range (2–5 mm) and notes leaf yellowing/abscission progression; NC State gives imperial size (<1/8 in) and underside appearance.

pathogen_scientific_name.quote: Southern bacterial wilt of tomato is caused by the soil-borne bacterium Ralstonia solanacearum (formerly known as Pseudomonas solanacearum )

type_of_disease.quote: caused by the soil-borne bacterium Ralstonia solanacearum

affected_parts.quote: Initial stages of the disease include a wilted appearance of the youngest leaves... the base of the plant may show brown cankers, root rot, and a cross section of an infected stem may show a brown discoloration of the vascular tissue

summary.value: Plants show wilting of the youngest leaves while still green, often appearing suddenly; initially infected plants wilt in the afternoon and recover in the evening, but as the disease progresses, the plant base develops brown cankers and root rot, the stem shows brown discoloration of vascular tissue and dark/light brown pith, and the plant eventually becomes permanently wilted and dies.
summary.quote: Initial stages of the disease include a wilted appearance of the youngest leaves. Typically, plants affected by bacterial wilt will show wilting symptoms while the plant is still green and can appear to come on suddenly. As the disease progresses, the base of the plant may show brown cankers, root rot, and a cross section of an infected stem may show a brown discoloration of the vascular tissue.

diagnostic_features.value: A freshly cut stem at the base of the plant placed in water shows a stream of a white slimy substance (bacterial streaming), a strong indicator of the bacterium in the vascular tissue; the interior of the stem is dark or light brown in the pith area.
diagnostic_features.quote: A freshly cut stem at the base of the plant placed in water can show a stream of a white slimy substance (fig. 2A) that is a strong indicator of the bacterium present in the vascular tissue (fig. 2B). The interior of the stem also can be a dark or light brown in the pith area (fig. 3).

look_alikes.quote: Bacterial wilt on tomato may be confused with other wilt or root rot diseases. Southern blight on tomato will also wilt, but affected plants will have a crown rot with white strands or mycelium growing up the stem from the soil line... Fusarium wilt of tomato will typically have one-sided leaf yellowing or wilting. Verticillium wilt typically causes V-shaped lesions on the leaflets and does not wilt as suddenly.

conflicts.note: UMD titles the disease 'Southern Bacterial Wilt of Tomato' (Race 1 of R. solanacearum); NC State uses 'Bacterial Wilt of Tomato' but acknowledges 'Southern bacterial wilt' synonym.
NC State includes Foliar (wilting of youngest leaves while still green); UMD's evidence focuses on stem/root/vascular and does not list foliar separately.
NC State notes the pathogen was 'formerly known as Pseudomonas solanacearum'; the current accepted name Ralstonia solanacearum is used as canonical.

pathogen_scientific_name.quote: Cladosporium leaf mold is a disease of tomato caused by the fungus Fulvia fulva (syn. Cladosporium fulvum).

type_of_disease.quote: Cladosporium leaf mold is a disease of tomato caused by the fungus Fulvia fulva (syn. Cladosporium fulvum).

affected_parts.quote: Disease symptoms are usually limited to foliage... Blossoms, stems, and fruit can be attacked by F. fulva... Contaminated seed can also initiate epidemics.

summary.value: Symptoms first appear as pale green or yellow spots on the upper sides of older leaves; the upper surface of affected leaves turns olive green, and leaves eventually curl, wither, and may drop. Affected fruit develops a black, leathery rot on the stem end.
summary.quote: Disease symptoms are usually limited to foliage, and first appear on the upper sides of older leaves as pale green or yellow spots. Leaf spots have irregular borders, but when infection is severe these spot coalesce and kill large areas of the foliage. The upper surface of affected leaves turns an olive green with more intense color near the center of lesions. Leaves eventually curl, wither, and may drop from the plant. Blossoms, stems, and fruit can be attacked by F. fulva. Affected fruit has a black, leathery rot on the stem end with an irregular margin.

diagnostic_features.value: The upper surface of affected leaves turns an olive green with more intense color near the center of lesions; affected fruit has a black, leathery rot on the stem end with an irregular margin.
diagnostic_features.quote: The upper surface of affected leaves turns an olive green with more intense color near the center of lesions... Affected fruit has a black, leathery rot on the stem end with an irregular margin.

conflicts.note: Cladosporium fulvum is listed in the source as a synonym of the canonical Fulvia fulva.

type_of_disease.quote: was determined to be of viral etiology in 1930

affected_parts.quote: Chlorotic or necrotic rings form on the leaves of many infected hosts ( Figure 4 ), and may also appear on the fruits of some hosts ( Figure 1 ). Necrosis may develop in the foliage of some hosts... Stunting is a common symptom of TSWV infection... it may cause the discoloration of seed produced on infected hosts

summary.value: Infected plants show bronzing on the upper sides of young leaves followed by distinct necrotic spots, with leaves cupped downward and tip dieback; ripe fruit develop chlorotic spots and blotches often with concentric rings, while green fruit show slightly raised areas with faint concentric zones.
summary.quote: Plants infected with Tomato spotted wilt virus exhibit bronzing of the upper sides of young leaves, which later develop distinct, necrotic spots . Leaves may be cupped downward. Some tip dieback may occur. On ripe fruit chlorotic spots and blotches appear, often with concentric rings. Green fruit show slightly raised areas with faint, concentric zones.

diagnostic_features.value: Bronzing of the upper sides of young leaves followed by distinct necrotic spots, with concentric rings/zones on fruit (chlorotic spots and blotches on ripe fruit; slightly raised areas with faint concentric zones on green fruit).
diagnostic_features.quote: exhibit bronzing of the upper sides of young leaves, which later develop distinct, necrotic spots . Leaves may be cupped downward. Some tip dieback may occur. On ripe fruit chlorotic spots and blotches appear, often with concentric rings. Green fruit show slightly raised areas with faint, concentric zones.

conflicts.note: UC IPM lists foliar plus fruit/pod symptoms (chlorotic spots/blotches on ripe fruit) but does not list seed or whole-plant; canonical taken from APS (rank 2) which mentions stunting and seed discoloration.
NMSU adds dark streaking of terminal stems (Stem) and stunting (Whole plant) but does not list seed; canonical taken from APS (rank 2).
NMSU titles the entry as the virus name; canonical is the disease name (without 'virus') as used by APS and UC IPM.

pathogen_scientific_name.quote: A second common fungal pathogen is Rhizopus stolonifer, which is in the bread mold group of fungi.

type_of_disease.quote: A second common fungal pathogen is Rhizopus stolonifer

summary.value: Water-soaked lesion that may exude clear liquid, with surface covered by thin, cotton-like fungal structures under humid conditions.
summary.quote: On tomatoes, Rhizopus rot appears water-soaked and may exude a clear liquid (Figure 5). The lesion surface may be covered with thin, cotton-like fungal structures (especially under humid conditions).

diagnostic_features.value: Tissues within the lesion are usually held together by relatively coarse strands of fungal hyphae, and dark sporulation may crown the white tuft of Rhizopus.
diagnostic_features.quote: Tissues within the lesion are usually held together by relatively coarse strands of fungal hyphae. Dark sporulation may crown the white tuft of Rhizopus .

summary.value: A circular rot that looks water-soaked, later darkening in the center and/or becoming overgrown with sparse, white mycelia.
summary.quote: Phytophthora , or buckeye rot , usually causes a circular rot that looks water-soaked, later darkening in the center and/or becoming overgrown with sparse, white mycelia (Figure 7).

diagnostic_features.value: Initial small surface stains enlarge in storage and the pathogen can move from diseased to adjacent healthy fruit, leading to "buckeye nesting" or "snowballs".
diagnostic_features.quote: However, in storage the small stains enlarge, and the pathogen can move from diseased to adjacent healthy fruit, leading to the term "buckeye nesting" or "snowballs"

look_alikes.quote: Phoma rot ( Phoma destructiva ) begins at the blossom end of fruit as sunken black spots with water-soaked edges and dark-centered rings, similar in appearance to buckeye rot.

type_of_disease.quote: Affected areas generally show white, cottony mycelium that soon produces large, irregularly shaped, black sclerotia

affected_parts.quote: Symptoms include water-soaked areas on flowers and at stem joints where senescent flower petals have fallen. The infection quickly kills stems, which eventually dry and take on a bleached appearance. Water-soaked stem lesions may also appear at the soil line ... Infected fruits turn gray and rot.

summary.value: White mold appears at flowering as water-soaked areas on flowers and stem joints; infected stems are killed, dry out and take on a bleached appearance, and affected areas show white, cottony mycelium with large black sclerotia. Infected fruits turn gray and rot.
summary.quote: White mold generally appears on tomato plants at flowering. Symptoms include water-soaked areas on flowers and at stem joints where senescent flower petals have fallen. The infection quickly kills stems, which eventually dry and take on a bleached appearance. ... Affected areas generally show white, cottony mycelium that soon produces large, irregularly shaped, black sclerotia. Infected fruits turn gray and rot.

diagnostic_features.value: White, cottony mycelium that produces large, irregularly shaped, black sclerotia; bleached appearance of dried killed stems; stem lesions show hollow pith when cut open with white moldy growth at the edges and small black sclerotia in or on the lesion.
diagnostic_features.quote: Lesions on the stem show hollow pith when cut open, and white moldy growth is usually visible at the edges of the lesions. Small black spherical fruiting bodies (sclerotia) are usually observed in or on the lesion.

look_alikes.quote: Timber rot, also known as Sclerotinia or white mold

conflicts.note: Texas A&M lists both S. sclerotiorum and S. minor as causal agents; UC IPM lists only S. sclerotiorum.
Same disease referred to as 'White Mold' by UC IPM and 'Timber Rot' by Penn State and Texas A&M.
